title: Making a home book press
subtitle: A simple pressing board and G-clamp setup for bookbinding
excerpt: A book press is the most useful piece of equipment in a home bindery. A commercial cast-iron nipping press costs a considerable amount; a functional equivalent can be made from two pieces of plywood and a pair of G-clamps for very little. This tutorial covers the materials, dimensions, and use of a simple home-made press.
sourceNotes: The plywood-and-clamp book press is a widely documented alternative to the cast-iron nipping press, recommended in multiple home-bookbinding guides from the late 20th century onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pair of flat, smooth boards placed on either side of a freshly glued text block or case to hold everything flat and even while adhesives dry.

content.text: A book press solves one practical problem: keeping a glued structure flat, under even pressure, while the adhesive dries. The commercial cast-iron nipping press does this with a screw mechanism and a precision-machined platen. A home-made version uses two pieces of smooth-faced plywood and two G-clamps. The difference is convenience and speed; the result is the same.

items.name: Two pieces of birch-faced plywood, 12 mm thick
items.notes: Cut to 350 mm x 250 mm. This covers A4 and smaller work. Sand the faces smooth. A coarser board transfers its texture to the work.

items.name: Two G-clamps, 100 mm
items.notes: One clamp at each end of the boards. 100 mm opening covers most text blocks up to 75 mm thick.

items.name: Waxed paper or silicone release paper
items.notes: Placed between the press boards and the work to prevent adhesive strike-through.

content.text: Sand both inner faces smooth with 120-grit sandpaper and then 220-grit. A rough face leaves impressions in soft materials. Seal the outer faces and edges with a coat of varnish or diluted PVA to prevent warping from moisture absorption. Allow to dry flat. A warped pressing board produces a warped book.
Place waxed paper between the work and both

to prevent adhesive bonding the book to the boards. Position the G-clamps at each end of the board stack, equidistant from the centre. Tighten both clamps alternately rather than fully tightening one before the other; uneven pressure causes the work to shift or bow. Tighten until firm resistance is felt in the clamp screw, not until the board deflects visibly.

Text block freshly glued and lined: 30 minutes minimum under the press, then released to air under a stack of books overnight. Newly cased book with PVA-glued endpapers: 4 hours in the press, overnight under weight. A leather-covered case where the boards have been dampened by the adhesive: overnight in the press, then a further day under weight before the book is stored or used.

items.symptom: The book has a slight bow after pressing
items.cause: The pressing boards are warped, or the clamps were tightened unevenly.
items.fix: Leave the book under a heavy stack of books overnight rather than in the press. If the bow is slight, it often relaxes on its own once the adhesive sets fully.

items.symptom: Adhesive has bonded the book to the pressing board
items.cause: The waxed paper was omitted or shifted during clamping.
items.fix: Work a bone folder gently under the book to separate it from the board. Damaged spots can be covered by the endpaper if caught early.
